title: Oliver Sacks: Gratitude Near the End
dek: The neurologist who made case histories human, then met terminal cancer with attention and gratitude.
mortality_event: Terminal metastatic ocular/uveal melanoma in 2015, after a 2006 eye tumor, before his final essays collected as Gratitude.
citation_passage: Oliver Sacks (1933–2015) was a neurologist and writer who turned clinical case histories into portraits of individual lives. His 1973 book Awakenings described patients who had survived the encephalitis lethargica epidemic and later received L-Dopa, establishing the humane narrative method that shaped his wider work. Sacks had a retinal melanoma that cost him vision in one eye; when cancer later spread, he announced in 2015 that his remaining time was limited. This obituary comic traces his habits of close attention, his refusal to reduce patients to diagnoses, the reach of Awakenings and later books, and his response to terminal illness. His final essays, collected as Gratitude, considered friendship, work, beauty, mortality, and the value of a singular life. The account draws on the official Oliver Sacks biography and Gratitude pages, plus obituaries from The Guardian and The New York Times.
page_summaries: Oliver Sacks develops the close attention that will shape his work as a neurologist and writer.
In the hospital ward, Sacks treats each patient as a person whose life exceeds any diagnosis.
Awakenings brings Sacks to a wide audience by showing illness as a story about lost and recovered time.
Sacks’s books give neurology a humane voice through accounts of memory, music, perception, and individual lives.
After cancer returns as a terminal illness in 2015, Sacks responds through the discipline of attention.
Sacks writes his final essays about friendship, work, beauty, mortality, and gratitude.
Sacks leaves readers a method: see the person rather than the label and keep the world vivid.
